All right, here we go. Topic of today is going to be sales cycles and essentially how you prospect. So let me explain what that is. So the idea is that when you have a straight line sale, right, which is every sales is straight line, doesn't matter what it is in person or over the phone, every sale takes on the shape of a straight line. Watch, here's the logic behind it. It's important you understand the logic. And actually, interesting, one of the things that really occurred to me this week when I was working for this company, and that's not just the correct way, I really reinforced it. And also I'm doing some stuff with Alex Mayer right now and helping him out with something. He helped each other stuff is that even with his stuff and I'm helping him, first thing I do is I always make sure that I've dug into the company into the idea itself enough that I can make what I call the screen from the hilltops airtight logical case. Remember, this is the key to using the straight line at the highest level. Just I promise you that you must take the time to really explain your product, your service, your good of service, right? And how you position it in with within your industry, why your product, why is yours the best? Why does it make so much sense to buy? And it can't just because it's a good deal or it's got good, it's got to be like using relating it to other industries, relating it to other other companies in the industry, really hitting on talking about really developing. I don't want to get to scripting training, but I just want you to understand that at the heart of what's going on, as you move someone down the straight line from the open to the close, yeah, you're creating certainty for the three tenths, no doubt, right? That's obvious. You're building massive certainty for the three tenths, but just don't ever forget the reason why that first question, when you get to the end of the front half of the sale and they give you that first objection, right? Because remember, after you ask for the order for the first time, there's three things that can happen. They could say yes, in which case you go right to the end, you close the one that's a no objection close, it's called the lay down and straight line parlance, right? They could say, and that's very rare, 3%, 5%, depending on the industry, it's usually pretty rare to sum up objection typically, right? We can't rely on that. It's not a large enough number. They could say no, which means they're not interested in which case you end the sale, but by the way, just so you know, and the reason I say end the sale is because these are so rare at this point to get a hard no, because think about what happened. You've already taken this person through an intelligence gathering session, right? You've been through either one or two calls and if they, the only way you would have said, well, based on what you said, this is a perfect fit for you, they would have answered all your questions. It would have given you essentially all their pain points, their needs. Someone that did all of that and was open to hearing about your idea enough to go into that in the first place, right? They're not going to, oh, no, not interested. That's not what they say. That not interested happens in the front half of the sale when you say, I'd like to share, before you even start to qualify, you have 60 seconds, I'd like to ask you some question, you got a minute, he says, no, not interested. That's when you get that in the very beginning over here before you even start asking questions. When you say, hey, I'm calling VEX, they're not interested. That's what market, that's a marketing function, right? Essentially whether you're, you know, it's telemarketing door to door, incoming leads, it doesn't matter what it might be. That's where you get the not interested, right? So not interested happens in the front over here when you ask for the order, you get not no, but you get maybe, as in maybe I will, maybe I won't. Let me think about it as maybe, let me call you back as maybe, bad time of year is maybe, and all those other objectives, they all fall to that maybe category, right? And no matter what happens here, remember that first point, that first question, let me ask you this, I hear what you're saying, but does the idea make sense to you? You like the idea and that is what that is relating to this first 10. Now as I said, all three 10s have to be established, right? They have to be, but that first 10, especially that logical aspect of the first 10, you cannot use the straight line at the highest level unless you've taken the time to really develop this case, promise you. And I think on some level why I out close most people, all people, I've never been next to it in any scenario, right? Is because I'm especially good, I really excel at this. I'm able to sort of look at a company and come up with a way of explaining what they do and explaining how the system works in the context of the entire globe, meaning all the business industries needs and make it awesome. So it's like, wow, it's fucking great, logically. And once I do that, once you've gotten someone, once you do that and you have it, it's sort of like, wow, it makes so much fucking sense. I'm a buying, but they love it logically, right? They get it. Everything else becomes possible. If you never get them to this high level of logical certainty, you're essentially trying to fight with one hand behind your back, seriously, because the logical mind serves as the human bullshit detector. So if you try to just sweep someone away with emotion and they're not 100% certain logically, it's like bullshit, bullshit, there's something wrong here, it just makes you get it. So this is the underpinning of the sale. They don't buy on logic, they buy on emotion. And they justify that logic, but without the logic, the emotional case is very hard to keep going because people think you're full of shit, basically. It's also why people sometimes will close and get an incredibly high cancellation or bounce rate, right? Why people cancels, because what happened was they get what you call buyer's remorse. Well, buyer's remorse is mostly a function of when you haven't established the first 10 and you got the device solely almost strictly on emotion. Because what happened is, guess what? Emotion unlike logic wears off. See you get sort of really emotionally certain 15 minutes later, a day later, they don't feel that way because you temporarily got there. Yet the logical case, you explain something to someone in a way that makes total sense, that sticks. It's a logical idea. People get that. Logical certainly wanes somewhat, but imagine if you get someone to attend logic and attend emotion, let's say you got them there, right? Logic emotion. The next morning, okay, this could be a back up to a five. This might be an 8.5 or nine. So they both wane somewhat, but logic lasts a lot longer because it's just you're thinking it through, you could line up all the elements in your mind, all right? So why am I saying this in terms of training about prospecting and sales cycles? The answer is, is that on some level, okay, all the sales cycle is, is that you've gone through your business long enough, you've tested ideas and you've figured out over how many calls does it take for me to essentially to most elegantly and really to most assuredly be able to line up these three 10s. And especially the logical solution, what's the adequate number of touch points I need to get my point across to someone? See it's not like there's any right or wrong answer, like one calls better than two, two is better than three. It's just the question is, is how many calls does it take for you to get to the point with someone else where you can line up these three elements in a single moment in time, starting with the logical case? Because without that, you're just, you're not using the straight line correctly. Now, there are different theories on this, meaning, you know, what's the best system, one call, two calls. Let me go through this in real detail and explain to you how you're going to, how you're going to expand or contract your prospecting session. That was his question, right? Based on the cycle. So watch what happens in a two call system. Okay, let's start with one call, in a one call system, right? One call. What that means is basically that the entire, the entire straight line is going to occur from front to back on one call. So you have the first four seconds, right? And from there you're going to go into the intelligence gather report building. Now remember just one thing here, when I say the first four seconds, remember there's a link four seconds and there's a 10 to 15 seconds where you say, you know, you don't say, hey, how you doing today? And you say a couple of cool questions. You got to say something about name my company, XYZ company, one of the fastest growing this reason for the call today is, and that's your bridge from the first four seconds. And you say, you know, if you have a few sets, I'd like to share it. I'd like to add, you know, like to send you some information and get back to you down the road. So let me show you a classic one call closes. You have 60 seconds, right? I'd like to share an idea with you. Got a minute? So after you go through these first four seconds, then that bridges to another 10 to 15 seconds and that 15 second pattern. In other words, the first four seconds is your absolute intro. Hey, Jordan, call me XYZ company. How you doing today? That's your first four seconds, right? Hey, it's John there. John, Jordan Balfour, call me XYZ company. How you doing today? And I'm talking fast now because I want to make sure we get this a little more now, right? You could talk a bit slower. Okay. Take a quick sip of rebel. How you doing today? They say, I'm okay. You say, great. Now, John, you might have heard of my company, XYZ. We're one of the fastest growing XYZ in this industry, right? And reason for the call today is something that just came across. We reach out to homeowners in your area, right? We have an incredible program, right? Blah, blah, blah, blah. If you have 60 seconds, I'd like to share an opportunity with you. You got a minute? Reason and momentum. So you got a minute and they say, yeah, sure. Why not? And you say, great. Now from here, you don't go in and you just say, yeah, I want to share an opportunity with you. You got a minute and they say, sure. So that would theoretically mean what? That you're going to now show them the idea, the opportunity, right? But that's not what we do. Instead, we say, great. Let me just ask you a couple of quick questions so I can get an idea of what best suits you. So I can understand, so I can, you know, so I don't waste your time. I don't, whatever you want, you know, you have your justifier so I don't waste your time. I can see what you need, your needs or whatever you say there. Right. So in other words, you say, I want to share an idea with you. You got a minute and they say, yeah, go ahead. You say, great. Let me just ask you a couple of questions first to make sure this is right for you. And then you go from there, whatever that pattern you use, right? So that's your next pattern right here that links. So you end this and then you go into your intelligence gathering. In other words, essentially you're asking them if they have time for you to share an idea with them, but then you don't go directly into sharing the idea. You still need to ask them questions. This is the big miss some people make in a one call close. You have to ask the questions in the same call. You're going to do a few, not doing any less of a prospecting call. If you're doing it all on one call, you don't collapse prospecting. Prospecting still must take place. Okay. Remember, sell me this pen. You ask questions, right? So in other words, in a one call close, you go from, I'd like to share an idea with you. You got a minute. They say, yes, a great. Let me ask you a couple of quick questions so I don't waste your time. And then you go through your questions one by one. Now remember this, I'm not going to teach you prospecting in the sense of what are the quite that's a different training. I'm talking about context here of sale cycle, right? So then you follow all your rules of straight line prospecting. I have to tell you that when I went through this, I think probably the most powerful part of the buddy was at this training with me, right? I'm sure buddy's on this, watching this right now. And you can tell you is that the most important thing I did for these people, I really think is that their method of asking questions was so confusing that they weren't ending the intelligence guy. These are smart guys. Just, you know, these are sharp guys, smart guys with a great product. I'm dead serious. So they had a good thing going, right? And but what they were doing is, is they were ending the prospecting session without really finding out what someone's pain points were, what their real primary need was. And because of that, when they transitioned into the main body of the presentation, it was really fucking awkward. In fact, they were doing role playing, right? I said they were actually doing role playing. They had an actual sale. They were doing a mock sale. And we got lucky because the person playing the customer did a really good job. And because the person who was doing the salesperson didn't ask the right questions like, do you have children? How old are they? Where do they go to school? Is education important to you? So because that was left out of the intelligence guy, the answer is not real estate, you know, school systems, right? Where are you going to buy a house, right? So because that was left out, the prospect goes, well, you know, I don't know, where's the schools? Where's the schools there? And he started interrupting the salesperson when he was presenting because the salesperson didn't ask him enough questions and didn't have a tail of the presentation as a result of that. So he made everything go, he lost control and he watched it play out in real time. My point is this, whether using a one-call system, a two-call system, or a three-call system, and I really don't recommend more than three-calls except in those cases where you're really in a complex sale where there's multiple decision makers or the stakes are not so much big money, but that if they buy, there's all sorts of people in a company that have to work together to make the technology work. Those typically take more than three calls because other people got to sign off and be made aware and be made certain as well. But barring those circumstances, your basic sales, three calls is typically the limit, my book, okay? And no matter what it is though, remember, so in a one-call close, right, what happens? So you're going to ask all these questions, right? And then you're going to say to them at the end, and remember, you follow all the rules of straight line prospecting, asking questions in the logical order, big picture to small picture, less invasive to more invasive, right? All the things that we've taught you, using your active listening as they respond, and then ending with a powerful transition right here based on what you said to me. What I had is a perfect few, so let me explain exactly what it is. And then you're going to go now and transition right into your proposal. Your solution. It's one call. So you said I want to share an opportunity with you. Then you ask them questions. They basically said this is a perfect few. Name of the company or name of the opportunity, name of the product, right? And fucking bam is your solution right here. And it doesn't matter what the words are, separate training, right? And then at the end of this, what do you do? You're going to ask for the order for the first time, following your energy in benefits out. Now the big thing that I saw was happening where they weren't really making the sale simple at the end. They weren't reminding the prospect of the benefits before they asked for the order. Then you ask for the order for the first time and you shut up. And you wait for your objection. And then we go into the back end of the sale and that's that, right? That's a one call close. And either now you're going to loop around here and either close the sale or you won't, right? And if you choose to work on callback, separate issue, it depends on the industry. We'll deal with that in a separate training. The answer is yes, usually there are some, but you don't want to get carried over because you start to lose your, you know, you get that yellow streak. Oh, if everyone, maybe they'll call back and I'll be nice. You can't be that nice. Okay. So sales, you have to have some context to make it happen in one call. All right. That's your basic one call close, right? It all happens on one call. First time speaking to the person, you close them. Now let's move to a two call close. Just be aware that's one of the reasons why we want to collapse the sale cycle down to as few calls as possible. I found that when you go beyond three calls, it's just fucking mayhem. Trying to get in touch with these people again and again, it's real unless you're in that special type of businesses that really need that many calls, you do not want to be in a call cycle beyond three. Now, Jim, I know you have to run this by your, you know, these two, the CEO. Why don't we do this, Jim? Um, I'd love to set up an appointment to meet them myself. You always want to try to get multiple decision makers on the call. So just, you know, in the first call, if you had a chance, there was multiple decision makers, you would try to get as many of those decision makers as possible on that second call. I don't want to belabor this point because it starts to get a little hay wipe. There's so many permutations that could happen here. The point is, is that remembering a multi in a three call system, what's going to happen is your close on the second call is going to always be to set up the third call and this on this third call, you want to have all the relevant decision makers. The perfect example is would be a call like a real estate sales, where you have to have the wife there or the husband because or else it's just not rational. Right. So on that third, the reason for a third call might be is that you just have to get them both in one spot to wrap it all up. And that will be so on the third call. So you'd end up and they say, I love this is great. Awesome. Great. So why would you let's set up an appointment now on next Tuesday? I'm going to be in your area on same shit. Monday, Tuesday, we, which one's better for you and your wife? They say, too great. I'll be there. We could go through all stuff. Dot the eyes cross these, make sure everything is perfect for both of you. And we can go forward. Does that sound good? They say, yeah, great. And that's how you end the call. So a three call system would apply like when you have to get two decision makers there and the third call is just about getting them both there together and bam. So that next call, you have to have all decision makers there because you don't want to go beyond three calls. It starts to get crazy unless it's one of those crazy products. That's super complicated and intrusive into the whole business. Okay. That makes sense. Right. Now here's what I want to show you now. I want to show you a trick here. This is what I call a great way you really can knock it out of the fucking park. What I call is my hybrid one call system. It's like a hybrid one slash two call system. And this is the magic, right? I've done this. I've implemented this with companies all over the world and it works so well. They almost like, but I explained a fucking laugh because it's so, it's pretty ingenious, right? It works really, really well. What you see happen very often is that in the real world of selling, you have very often you have two different types of people making the calls. You have openers and you have closers. You have prospectors. You have professional sales. We will really out there just closing and I, I'm a proponent of that to a point. I'm a proponent for that. If you're the owner, but if I'm talking to you, the salesman, I'm wanting to find what you fucking just prospecting. In other words, what I don't want you to do is fall into the trap of people. So you're just a prospector because you are with me here and I'm teaching you this real fucking shit here. You are fucking not a prospect. You're a fucking closer. Closers make money. Now, if you're an owner, we talk differently to you, right? If you're an owner, it's really good. You want to separate out some people to be prospectors and some people to be closers because when you recruit new salespeople, it's a perfect way to get leads for your existing sales force. If you're expanding, you hire people, they get leads, they grow, they learn, they get mentored. Then when they have enough behind them under their belt, they can graduate. So you create a system where they can become closers if they do a good job prospecting. But now I'll go back. If you're just a prospect, be a fucking closer, right? There you go. So now let me tell you the system, how it works, right? You have a one call close, which is your, obviously your most efficient call before close because why? Because of this issue of getting in touch with the same person more than once. It, I assure you it is not easy and it can be maddening of just getting that person on the phone or in the office the second time, right? So the, what I found over the years, many, many years is the ultimate solution to this. And I started doing it at the, towards the end of stratton. Okay. When we, you know, it just started getting so, we were so good at, at the straight line, right? And it really started really rocking and rolling, right? I think we, you know, two or three, we just like, we just, we're just so big already, right? That people see, we started experimenting with stuff. So it's a hybrid one call slash two call system.
